in ONAP, we prefer to structure "if" statements in a way that simple stuff is done first (e.g., error checks) and then the code immediately returns, eliminating the need to indent/nest the more complex stuff that follows.  The alternative is to extract methods so that none of the branches is more than a few lines long (i.e., 3-5).

In a sense, what we would like to do is add methods to the 'ControlLoopEvent' classes, in order to do event-specific operations, like build a compatible notification object. However, that would be inappropriate, because 'ControlLoopEvent' and it's subclasses define message interfaces, and shouldn't be tied to one of the possible implementations.

'OnsetAdapter' is basically an extension class to 'ControlLoopEvent'. The hierarchy of subclasses under 'OnsetAdapter' should roughly match the hierarchy under 'ControlLoopEvent', although a perfect match isn't required. That's what the code in 'OnsetAdapter.get' is trying to do -- find the best match using Java's class rules. The table 'OnsetAdapter.map' contains the explicitly-registered entries, inserted via 'OnsetAdapter.register', but also contains best-matches discovered by the code in 'OnsetAdapter.get'. The algorithm starting at line 70 in 'OnsetAdapter.get' is only run once for each class that is not explicitly registered. I suppose we could simplify 'OnsetAdapter.get' by requiring that every subclass be explicitly registered, but I wanted to make it a bit smarter than that.

If you had a table mapping the 'ControlLoopEvent' subclass to 'ControlLoopNotification' subclass, how would you use that to construct a 'ControlLoopNotification' instance? Would you use the reflection interface to locate a constructor with a 'ControlLoopEvent' (or subclass) argument? Wouldn't it be cleaner and more flexible to have a table of 'ControlLoopNotificationFactory' instances? That's what 'OnsetAdapter.createNotification' does. I suppose we could have named 'OnsetAdapter' as 'ControlLoopNotificationFactory', but we might want to add some 'ControlLoopEvent'-specific operations to it at some point. I prefer to think of it as an extension class to 'ControlLoopEvent'.

This method exists, so it can be called on by the 'Actor' subclasses to ensure that the 'OnsetAdapter.map' table contains the entries it depends upon. The whole idea was to make this extensible -- 'ClassLoader' is used to discover the 'Actor' subclasses, and they call the 'register' methods to ensure that the table is populated. If just referencing 'VirtualOnsetAdapter' in the 'Actor' subclasses were sufficient to trigger static initialization in 'VirtualOnsetAdapter', we would place the 'register' call within a 'static { ...}' block -- unfortunately, this doesn't work.
Done
I have changed the code to call 'VirtualOnsetAdapter.register()', and changed it to fetch 'virtualOnsetAdapter' from the table in 'OnsetAdapter'.

It was commented out when using ONAP's version of 'ControlLoopEvent', which is abstract (the older version we had was not abstract). I uncommented the code, and called 'new ControlLoopEvent() {}' to create a subclass, and get around the 'abstract' problem.

I don't think this should be a 'Transaction' method -- adjuncts exist in order to extend the base functionality provided by 'Transaction'. You could argue that this class should be in a separate project, rather than 'base'.

I don't think this should be a 'GuardContext' method -- 'GuardContext' was something we used with the older model and 'm2' ('GuardContext' is under the 'm2' project here, although it wasn't in our environment).

I don't think this should be a new 'GuardAdjunct' constructor -- the creation of the 'GuardAdjunct' should be an implementation detail to the caller. If you look at the call to 'GuardAdjunct.create(...)' in 'M2CLRulevUSPLCMGuardTemplate.drl':

GuardAdjunct.create(transaction, $context.getGuardContext());

I think the current call is preferable to doing the following:

(void) new GuardAdjunct(transaction, $context.getGuardContext());

I believe this should remain here as a static method.

I did change the type to 'Map'.

My reaction to '*Manager' classes is similar to your reaction to 'static' fields and methods -- I feel they are something to be avoided. For reasons of encapsulation, it seems that the purpose of an OnsetAdapter and the way OnsetAdapters are selected are tightly coupled, and there is no need for a parallel singleton class. If you look at standard Java library classes dealing with similar situations, 'Locale', for instance, there are static methods to fetch entries from the internal table, as well as methods to modify the internal table.
Doesn't 'Whitebox' give you the ability to tweak the table if needed?
Done

The algorithm below should only get run once for each 'ControlLoopEvent' subclass, as it is used for the first time. After that point, the table won't change for the lifetime of the VM (or the lifetime of the Drools session, if this class is dynamically loaded). I changed the method so it returns 'rval' immediately if 'rval != null' to handle the high-runner case up front -- no need to build a 'Function' object for 'computeIfAbsent'.

The change I made above reduces the degree of nesting by one, which should reduce the Sonar threat. Actually, there is a general algorithm here, and maybe it should be placed in the 'common' repository, or maybe it already exists somewhere. I also need it in server pool.

It would be equivalent to the algorithm that the Java compiler uses to find the best match among a set of single-argument methods that have the same name. You want to find a method where the argument exactly matches the class of the object you are passing, or the one that is the closest match. It may be that you have more than one match (ambiguous method reference, equivalent to the "multiple matches" on line 118), or you may have no matches (equivalent to line 116). Ideally, you have one best match.

The utility method could have the signature: static Set<Class> findMatches(Set<Class> classes, Class targetClass)

It would then be up to the caller to decide what to do if the returned Set does not have a size of 1. It would also be up to the caller to cache the result in table somewhere, if needed, for performance reasons.
Done

I ended up changing 'clazz' to 'final', and referencing 'clazz' within the lambda function. I suspect there is a performance cost to creating and using the lambda function over inline code, because you are probably still using a 'synchronized' block internally when you use 'computeIfAbsent', and you have the lambda overhead. Also, you are are still creating the lambda object, even in cases where it isn't being invoked.

  new GA(...).attach(transaction)

  new GA().attach(...)

I do acknowledge that static methods can be difficult to override if you need to in a Junit test. Still, I don't like the complexity and lack of encapsulation that external auxiliary classes introduce.

I have made a change that provides a way to override the static method in a Junit test, without losing the benefits of encapsulation, and without adding complexity to the external interface. There is an additional class that has been introduced, but it is a nested class, and the complexity is only visible externally if there is a real need to override the method in a Junit test.

I am actually not too thrilled about the trend that I now see in the ONAP code. There are classes that are singletons, but that functionality winds up in three separate classes. You might has something like:

Xyz - an interface class with the operations that the singleton is providing
XyzManager - the one-and-only implementation of the Xyz interface
XyzConstants - yet another class, whose primary contribution is a static 'getManager()' method with return type 'Xyz' that returns an instance of 'XyzManager'.

I see this as unnecessary complexity. Why define an interface class if there is really only one implementation? Also, I believe the 'XyzConstants' class was only introduced because Sonar didn't like the static 'getManager()' method and associated field that used to exist in 'Xyz'.

If you like the 'getManager()' approach, you could still do this with a single class 'Xyz'. You could put your static 'getManager()' method there, and make all of the other methods instance methods. If a Junit test needs to override some of the functionality, they could subclass 'Xyz', override the methods they need to, and leave the rest intact. They could then overwrite the static 'manager' field with 'Whitebox' to replace the singleton for the duration of their Junit tests. I don't think this approach offends Sonar in any way, but I am not certain.

per decision, this should be made into a regular method (which may or may not entail moving to a different class).

I agree with your comments about having multiple classes for one thing, and yes, XyzConstants was only introduced because of a sonar complaint.  If there is always only one implementation intended, then we could move getManager() from XyzConstants to XyzManager and rename it getInstance().  As for having both a class and an interface when only one implementation will ever be used, the only advantage I see to that is that it can eliminate a circular dependency - having getManager() appear within XyzConstants also helps to eliminate circular dependencies.

FYI: the test approach you mentioned that overwrites the static 'manager' field with 'Whitebox' is exactly the approach that's used.
